meta.description: What Amazon interviewers look for: positive and negative signals by interview type and level. Amazon interviews uniquely blend Leadership Principles (LPs) into every round.

dimensions.name: Problem Solving
dimensions.notes: Amazon coding rounds are 60 minutes: ~15 min LP behavioral warm-up + ~45 min coding. Pacing awareness matters.
positive_signals: Clarifies problem before coding — asks about constraints, edge cases, input size
Manages the 45-minute coding window well — doesn't over-invest in clarification at the expense of implementation
Thinks through multiple approaches and articulates trade-offs before committing
Identifies optimal time/space complexity and explains the reasoning
Breaks problem into sub-problems naturally
Handles edge cases proactively (empty input, single element, overflow, large scale)
Can adapt when initial approach hits a wall — pivots efficiently
negative_signals: Jumps straight into coding without understanding the problem
Poor pacing — spends too long on approach discussion with insufficient time for implementation
Gets stuck on one approach and can't pivot
Misses obvious edge cases even after prompting
Cannot analyze time/space complexity of own solution
Needs heavy hints to reach even a brute-force solution

dimensions.name: Code Quality
positive_signals: Clean, readable code with meaningful variable names
Modular — extracts helper functions naturally
Consistent style and formatting
Handles errors and edge cases in code, not just verbally
Code runs correctly on first or second attempt
Uses language idioms and standard library appropriately
Code reflects 'Insist on the Highest Standards' — production quality, not hacky
negative_signals: Spaghetti code — deeply nested, long functions
Single-letter variables everywhere
Code doesn't compile or has many syntax errors
Copy-paste duplication instead of abstraction
Ignores error handling entirely
Over-engineers with unnecessary complexity

dimensions.name: Communication
positive_signals: Thinks aloud — interviewer can follow the reasoning throughout
Explains WHY they chose an approach, not just what they're doing
Asks clarifying questions at the right moments
Acknowledges trade-offs between approaches
Responds well to hints — incorporates them smoothly without ego
Tests own code by tracing through examples verbally
Transitions smoothly from the LP warm-up to coding
negative_signals: Silent for long stretches — interviewer has no idea what they're thinking
Can't explain own code when asked
Ignores interviewer's hints or suggestions
Defensive when approach is questioned
Over-explains trivial things, under-explains complex ones

dimensions.name: Testing & Verification
positive_signals: Tests with normal cases, edge cases, and corner cases
Dry-runs code mentally before declaring done
Catches own bugs during review
Suggests how they would write unit tests
Considers performance at scale — does the solution hold up with large inputs?
negative_signals: Says 'I think this works' without testing
Only tests the happy path
Can't identify bugs when pointed to them
No consideration of how the solution performs at scale

dimensions.name: LP Warm-up Integration
dimensions.notes: The first ~15 minutes of each coding round are dedicated to 1-2 Leadership Principle behavioral questions. Performance here is factored into the overall round assessment.
positive_signals: Delivers concise, impactful STAR stories in the allotted time
Stories demonstrate the assigned LPs with specific, concrete examples
Transitions cleanly from behavioral to coding — no time wasted
LP responses show genuine alignment, not rehearsed talking points
negative_signals: LP stories are vague or don't demonstrate the principle asked about
Takes too long on behavioral — eats into coding time
Stories feel rehearsed and generic — no personal depth
Cannot connect experiences to specific Leadership Principles

level_calibration.SDE I: Solve 1-2 medium problems cleanly within the 45-minute window. Brute-force OK if they discuss optimal. Solid LP stories showing ownership and learning.
level_calibration.SDE II: Solve mediums quickly, make progress on hards. Must reach optimal for mediums. Clean code. LP stories showing cross-team impact.
level_calibration.Senior SDE: Solve hards. Discuss trade-offs deeply. Production-quality code. LP stories demonstrating leadership and broad organizational impact.
level_calibration.Principal SDE: Solve hards efficiently with elegant solutions. System-level thinking in algorithmic choices. Exemplary code. LP stories showing company-wide impact and strategic thinking.

dimensions.name: Customer Obsession & Deliver Results
positive_signals: Starts reasoning from customer needs — works backwards from the customer experience
Has concrete examples of customer-driven decisions that required trade-offs
Quantifies results with customer impact metrics (satisfaction, adoption, reduced friction)
Shows bias for action — made decisions with imperfect information to serve customers faster
Distinguishes reversible from irreversible decisions and acts accordingly
Persistent through obstacles to deliver customer value
negative_signals: Internally focused — optimizes for engineering elegance over customer value
Describes activities not results — no measurable customer impact
Analysis paralysis — waits for perfect information while customers suffer
Gives up when things get hard — no evidence of persistence
Can't articulate how their work affected real customers

dimensions.name: Ownership & Dive Deep
positive_signals: Goes beyond job description — took on problems others avoided
Thinks about long-term consequences, not just short-term delivery
Digs into root causes — doesn't accept surface-level explanations
Uses data to verify assumptions — skeptical when metrics and narratives differ
Holds high quality standards and pushes the team to meet them
Owns failures as well as successes — doesn't say 'that's not my job'
negative_signals: Stays in lane — only does what's assigned
Blames other teams for failures
Accepts surface-level explanations without digging deeper
No evidence of quality standards beyond 'good enough'
Trusts reports and dashboards without verification

dimensions.name: Invent, Simplify & Think Big
positive_signals: Found simple solutions to complex problems — reduced unnecessary complexity
Proposed ambitious solutions — thought beyond incremental improvements
Demonstrates continuous learning — actively expands their knowledge
Challenges the status quo with data-backed alternatives
Balances innovation with pragmatism — creative but ships
Applies knowledge from outside their immediate domain
negative_signals: Over-engineers — adds complexity without justification
Small, safe goals only — no evidence of ambitious thinking
Stagnant skillset — hasn't learned anything new recently
Accepts status quo without questioning — 'that's how it's always been done'
Innovation without execution — ideas that never shipped

dimensions.name: Earn Trust & Have Backbone
positive_signals: Transparent communication — admits mistakes openly and early
Voices disagreement respectfully with evidence, not ego
Commits fully to decisions once made — even ones they disagreed with
Seeks disconfirming data — doesn't just look for evidence that supports their view
Builds genuine trust through consistent reliability and candor
Gives difficult but constructive feedback when needed
negative_signals: Goes along to get along — never pushes back
Undermines decisions after the fact — doesn't truly commit
Overconfident without evidence — can't admit being wrong
Avoids difficult conversations — conflict-averse
Takes credit for others' work
Says one thing and does another

dimensions.name: Hire, Develop & Team Building
mapped_lps: Strive to be Earth's Best Employer
positive_signals: Invests in others' growth with specific, tailored mentoring
Raises the hiring bar — has clear criteria and refuses to settle
Creates an inclusive and psychologically safe team environment
Accomplishes goals with limited resources — creative under constraints
Gives actionable feedback that helps people improve
Promotes diversity and different perspectives on the team
negative_signals: Doesn't invest in team development
Threatened by strong team members — hoards information
Throws resources at problems without prioritization
No evidence of mentoring impact — vague 'I helped them'
Ignores team dynamics and wellbeing

dimensions.name: Bar Raiser Assessment
dimensions.notes: Unique to Amazon. The Bar Raiser is a specially trained interviewer from outside the hiring team who ensures the candidate raises the bar (is better than 50% of current employees at that level). This dimension is evaluated holistically across all LP signals.
positive_signals: Would clearly raise the bar for the team — demonstrably better than the average performer at this level
Strong across multiple LPs, not just one dimension
Shows trajectory — evidence of rapid growth and increasing impact over time
Unique strengths that would add something the team currently lacks
Consistent quality of examples — not relying on a single standout story
Would thrive in Amazon's culture — comfortable with ambiguity, ownership, high standards
negative_signals: Would be average at best — doesn't clearly raise the bar
Strong on one LP but weak on several others — inconsistent profile
Stories are too similar — limited range of experiences
No evidence of growth trajectory — has plateaued
Cultural misfit signals — uncomfortable with ownership, high standards, or fast pace
Would need significant coaching on basics for this level

level_calibration.SDE I: Clear STAR stories with personal ownership. Demonstrates Customer Obsession and Learn and Be Curious. Growth mindset. Raises the bar for entry-level.
level_calibration.SDE II: Deeper impact stories with quantified results. Cross-team collaboration. Strong Ownership and Dive Deep examples. Beginning leadership signals.
level_calibration.Senior SDE: Leadership stories required — influence, mentoring, technical direction. Demonstrates Earn Trust and Have Backbone at scale. Bar Raiser expects organizational impact.
level_calibration.Principal SDE: Must show company-level strategic impact. Thinks Big with examples that shaped direction. Hire and Develop the Best at team/org level. Bar Raiser expects top-of-level performance across all LPs.

dimensions.name: Requirements Gathering
positive_signals: Asks clarifying questions BEFORE designing — works backwards from customer requirements
Distinguishes functional vs non-functional requirements clearly
Identifies key constraints: scale, latency, consistency, availability, cost
Scopes the problem — explicitly states what's in and out of scope
Estimates scale: users, QPS, storage, bandwidth with reasonable assumptions
Frames requirements in terms of customer experience and SLAs
negative_signals: Starts drawing architecture immediately without gathering requirements
Designs for the wrong scale
Never asks about non-functional requirements or SLAs
Tries to design everything at once — no scoping

dimensions.name: High-Level Design & Service Architecture
positive_signals: Clear service-oriented architecture with well-defined service boundaries
Data flow is obvious from the diagram — clear API contracts between services
Identifies the right services — follows single responsibility principle at service level
API design is clean with appropriate choice of sync vs async communication
Data model supports the requirements and access patterns efficiently
Thinks in terms of AWS/cloud primitives naturally (but not required to name specific services)
Considers service ownership and team boundaries
negative_signals: Monolithic design with no clear service boundaries
Missing critical components — no clear data flow
API design is an afterthought with no contract clarity
Data model doesn't support the required query patterns
Services are either too coarse-grained or too fine-grained with no reasoning

dimensions.name: Deep Dives & Trade-offs
positive_signals: Proactively discusses trade-offs: consistency vs availability, latency vs throughput, cost vs reliability
Goes deep on the most critical service — doesn't stay surface-level everywhere
Considers failure modes: what happens when a service goes down? Circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks
Discusses caching strategy with invalidation and consistency implications
Addresses data partitioning with clear reasoning about partition keys and hot spots
Considers idempotency, exactly-once semantics, and distributed transaction patterns
Thinks about system evolution and backward compatibility of APIs
negative_signals: Stays surface-level — 'we'll use DynamoDB' without explaining why or how
Cannot discuss trade-offs when asked
Name-drops AWS services without understanding their properties and limitations
No consideration of failure modes or degraded operation
Sharding strategy doesn't match access patterns
No awareness of distributed systems challenges (partial failures, network partitions)

dimensions.name: Scalability, Cost & Operational Excellence
positive_signals: Addresses horizontal scaling with specific strategies for each tier
Discusses monitoring, alerting, dashboards, and runbooks
Considers cost optimization — right-sizes resources, uses appropriate storage tiers
Mentions load balancing with appropriate strategy
Addresses deployment and rollout strategy (canary, feature flags, rollback plan)
Considers operational burden — on-call, debugging, log aggregation
Thinks about blast radius and failure isolation
negative_signals: Design works at toy scale but breaks at production scale
No mention of monitoring or operational readiness
Single points of failure not addressed
No consideration of cost — over-provisions everything
No deployment or rollback strategy

dimensions.name: Communication & Driving
positive_signals: Drives the conversation — interviewer doesn't have to pull teeth
Structures the discussion: requirements, high-level design, deep dive on critical path
Uses diagrams effectively — clear, organized, service-oriented
Responds to interviewer redirects gracefully
Explains decisions in terms of customer impact and business requirements
negative_signals: Waits for interviewer to ask every question — no ownership of the discussion
Unstructured rambling — jumps between topics randomly
Diagrams are messy — interviewer can't follow the architecture
Gets defensive when interviewer challenges a decision

level_calibration.SDE I: Not typically tested on system design at SDE I level.
level_calibration.SDE II: Solid high-level design with clear service boundaries. Basic trade-offs. Reasonable data model. May need prompts for deep dives. Aware of operational concerns.
level_calibration.Senior SDE: Drives the whole discussion. Deep on critical services. Production-ready thinking with SOA patterns. Discusses monitoring, cost, and operational excellence.
level_calibration.Principal SDE: Staff-level depth: capacity planning, multi-region architecture, system evolution over years, cross-org service platform design. Cost optimization at scale. Organizational implications of technical decisions.

dimensions.name: Problem Formulation
positive_signals: Translates business problem into a precise ML task (classification, ranking, regression, etc.)
Defines clear objective function — what exactly are we optimizing?
Identifies appropriate offline metrics (AUC, NDCG, precision@k) AND online metrics (CTR, revenue, engagement)
Considers whether ML is the right approach vs simpler alternatives
Discusses multi-objective trade-offs (relevance vs diversity, engagement vs quality)
Works backwards from the customer experience to define success
negative_signals: Jumps to model architecture without defining the problem
Can't articulate what the model is predicting or optimizing
Only discusses offline metrics, ignores online/business metrics
Uses ML when rules or heuristics would work better
Single objective with no consideration of trade-offs

dimensions.name: Data & Features
positive_signals: Identifies relevant data sources and their characteristics
Thoughtful feature engineering — explains WHY each feature helps
Addresses label generation: how are training labels obtained?
Considers feature freshness: real-time vs batch features
Discusses data quality, bias, sampling strategy, and class imbalance
Mentions feature stores and feature sharing across models
Addresses cold-start problem for new users/items
negative_signals: Assumes perfect labeled data exists
Lists features without explaining their relevance
Ignores data quality or bias issues
No consideration of feature computation cost or freshness
Doesn't address cold-start scenarios

dimensions.name: Model Architecture
positive_signals: Starts simple (logistic regression, GBDT) and explains when to add complexity
Justifies architecture choices for the specific problem
Discusses model capacity vs data size trade-off
Considers multi-stage architecture where appropriate (retrieval then ranking)
Addresses embedding strategies for sparse features
Discusses training: loss function, batch size, learning rate, distributed training
negative_signals: Jumps to the most complex model without justification
Can't explain why they chose a specific architecture
No progression from simple baseline to complex model
Ignores practical constraints: latency, compute cost, training time

dimensions.name: Serving & Infrastructure
positive_signals: Discusses serving latency requirements and how to meet them
Addresses model size, inference optimization, and cost at scale
Considers batch vs real-time prediction trade-offs
Mentions model versioning, rollback, and safe deployment
Discusses A/B testing and experimentation framework
Considers the ML infrastructure as a service — reusability across teams
negative_signals: Ignores serving entirely — only discusses training
No consideration of inference latency, throughput, or cost
Can't discuss safe model deployment
No mention of A/B testing or experimentation

dimensions.name: Monitoring & Iteration
positive_signals: Discusses model monitoring: data drift, prediction drift, metric degradation
Addresses feedback loops and their effects on model performance
Mentions retraining strategy and model freshness
Considers fairness and bias monitoring
Discusses the full iteration cycle: hypothesis, experiment, analysis, ship
Thinks about operational excellence for ML systems — on-call, alerting, auto-remediation
negative_signals: Train once, deploy forever mentality
No monitoring strategy
Ignores feedback loops entirely
No consideration of model fairness or safety
No plan for iterative improvement

level_calibration.SDE I: Basic ML formulation. Reasonable features. Simple model choice with justification. Understands train/eval split.
level_calibration.SDE II: End-to-end system. Good feature engineering. Model progression from simple to complex. Basic serving discussion. Cost-aware.
level_calibration.Senior SDE: Deep on all dimensions. Multi-objective. Real-time features. Monitoring. Experimentation. Service-oriented ML infrastructure.
level_calibration.Principal SDE: Platform-level thinking. Multi-model ecosystems. Efficiency at scale. ML strategy. Cross-org infrastructure and standards.

dimensions.name: Problem Decomposition for AI Delegation
positive_signals: Breaks problem into well-scoped sub-tasks BEFORE using AI tools
Identifies which parts to delegate to AI vs code manually — clear reasoning
Creates a clear plan and architecture before generating any code
Delegates boilerplate and scaffolding to AI, owns core logic and design
negative_signals: Throws the entire problem at AI in one prompt
No decomposition — random, unstructured prompting
Delegates the complex parts to AI and manually codes the trivial parts
No plan — just starts prompting

dimensions.name: Prompting Effectiveness
positive_signals: Writes clear, specific prompts with constraints and context
Iterates on prompts when output isn't right — adjusts rather than repeating
Provides examples or test cases in prompts when helpful
Specifies language, framework, and style preferences upfront
negative_signals: Vague prompts with no constraints or context
Repeats the exact same prompt expecting different results
Prompts are more verbose than the code they need
Over-prompts for trivial operations

dimensions.name: Critical Evaluation of AI Output
positive_signals: Reads and understands every line of AI-generated code before using it
Catches bugs, edge cases, or security issues in AI output
Modifies and improves AI output rather than accepting it wholesale
Questions AI's design choices and makes better ones
Tests AI-generated code with edge cases
negative_signals: Copy-pastes AI output without reading it
Can't explain what the AI-generated code does when asked
Trusts AI output blindly without verification
Accepts suboptimal code without improving it
Cannot debug when AI-generated code fails

dimensions.name: Architecture Ownership
positive_signals: Drives overall system design — AI fills in implementation details
Makes deliberate technology and API choices
Maintains consistent architecture across AI-generated components
Refactors AI output to fit the overall design and standards
negative_signals: AI drives the architecture — candidate follows wherever it goes
Inconsistent patterns across components
No opinion on design decisions — accepts whatever AI suggests
Final code looks like a patchwork of disconnected AI generations

dimensions.name: Scope & Velocity
positive_signals: Accomplishes significantly more than would be possible without AI
Uses time savings to add depth — tests, error handling, monitoring
Efficient workflow — smooth switching between manual coding and AI
Delivers a working, integrated solution
negative_signals: Achieves less than a no-AI interview despite having AI tools
Spends more time fighting with AI than coding productively
Solution is half-finished or doesn't integrate
AI assistance adds chaos rather than velocity

level_calibration.SDE I: Uses AI to handle boilerplate effectively. Understands generated code. Delivers a working solution.
level_calibration.SDE II: Strategic AI usage. Catches AI errors. Delivers a polished solution with good architecture.
level_calibration.Senior SDE: Masterful AI workflow. Deep critical evaluation. Production-quality output. Ambitious scope enabled by AI leverage.
level_calibration.Principal SDE: AI multiplier — uses AI to operate at a scope demonstrating principal-level system thinking and organizational impact.
